chapters.title: Inside you, every system talks to the others
chapters.text:
You may think your gut only handles eating and going to the bathroom, but your body works like a well tuned orchestra, even if some of the sounds are less than melodic. In fact, your organs run on so little energy that they need no more than a 100 watt light bulb.

People write poems about the heart and the brain, praising their sharpness and depth, but the gut often gets treated as nothing more than a hallway between the kitchen and the toilet.

Your gut is really a multi tool that shapes your energy, your mood, and even how you look.

Researchers now call the gut the next frontier of science. The bacteria living inside you quietly shape your nerves and your state of mind. A daily dose of Bifidobacterium bifidum, for instance, can lower stress in people who do not sleep enough. Scientists have only scratched the surface, but the early findings already show that your bowels have a large effect on overall health.

What if the secret to feeling good has been growing in your belly all along? Have you been treating it like the star it really is? Read on to learn how to care for your gut, then check the Try this section for a simple checklist.

A fact worth noting is that the surface area of the digestive system is roughly a hundred times larger than that of your skin.

chapters.title: A tour of your digestive tract
chapters.text:
Let us start small, with the mouth. Inside your mouth sit the papillae, tiny bumps on the inner cheeks that trigger saliva on demand. Your salivary glands put out up to three US pints, or about one and a half liters, of saliva each day.

A fun detail is that saliva contains a natural painkiller called opiorphin, which is stronger than morphine. You need it because the mouth is full of sensitive nerve endings. Some early research also suggests opiorphin may lift mood.

A quick honor roll of your body: the jaw muscles are the strongest, the tongue is the most flexible striated muscle, and tooth enamel is the hardest material in the body. And that is only the front door of the digestive system.

Further down, the esophagus is a clever tube. It keeps you from burping with every step after a big meal. Its shape also produces a small burst of gas if you lose control of the outer sphincter during a laughing fit. If you have ever had an air bubble trapped in your stomach, the easiest way to release it is to lie on your left side.

Many people treat burping and flatulence as rude, but holding it in is a bad idea. Trapped gas presses on nerves in the gut and causes discomfort and dizziness.

Stress and anxiety can keep your stomach from stretching the way it should, which leaves you feeling full or even sick. When relaxed, the stomach is roomier than most people realize.

To avoid heartburn, sit up straight or stand after a meal so the esophagus stays open.

If you could climb inside your gut, you would see a soft, velvety surface made of villi, microvilli, and glycocalyx folds. Without all those wrinkles, your small intestine would stretch nearly four and a half miles, or about seven kilometers.

The intestines are that long because the body needs to pull as much nutrition as possible from food. Once the small intestine grinds the meal into small pieces, the bacteria in the large intestine handle the leftovers.

chapters.title: What goes in is not always what you expect
chapters.text:
There is no shortage of diet advice, and the list keeps growing as research moves forward. So what really happens to food inside you? Knowing the basics makes it easier to choose what to eat.

A strange but true detail: if you timed it right, vomiting up a raw egg would give you something close to scrambled eggs, cooked by your stomach. The acid inside helps unfold proteins the same way heat does. Still, it is smarter to cook your meals to help digestion and lower the risk of getting sick.

When good bacteria process your food, they make small sugar molecules that feed your cells. You can also get those molecules straight from eating sugar, but the natural path is usually faster and smoother.

Your sweet tooth exists for a reason. Sugar is a quick source of energy, and the body loves that. The harm is not the sweetness itself but the source. After a sweet snack, your energy dips and hunger returns quickly.

When the body gets more sugar than it needs, it stores the rest for later. The most efficient way to store sugar is to turn it into fat, which is why a steady flow of simple sugars can lead to weight gain. A better choice is food with complex sugar chains that take longer to break down, such as wholegrain bread.

Not all fat is bad. A good fat like olive oil or butter protects your cells and nerves and can sharpen thinking.

Fats travel straight to your heart, without stopping at the liver the way most other nutrients do. To support your heart, try a few simple swaps.

Extra virgin olive oil is good for your muscles, eyes, and arteries, but it is not a cooking fat. Heat changes its fatty acids, so use butter or coconut oil for the stove. Rapeseed, linseed, and hempseed oils bring compounds that may help with inflammation, headaches, and menstrual pain when eaten as part of a balanced diet.

In the end, picking the right foods, building habits that feed your gut microbes, and learning how your insides work all point to better health. The Try this section at the end gives you a clear starting plan.

chapters.title: A short talk about going to the bathroom
chapters.text:
Have you ever thought of a regular bathroom trip as a small performance by two nervous systems? Probably not, but it really is.

Your outer sphincter works with your conscious brain and opens or closes on command. The inner sphincter runs on autopilot and looks after your inner well-being.

When the inner sphincter relaxes to open, it sends a signal to sensor cells sitting between the two sphincters. Those cells check whether the matter is gas or solid, then pass a message up to the brain. Your brain takes a quick read of the situation, decides you are on a first date, and tells the sphincter to hold on until you can reach a more private room.

The outer sphincter tightens on cue, and the inner sphincter accepts the deal. Sooner or later, the two try again. The constant back and forth between the two sphincters is a small dance between the needs of your bowels and the world around you.

Listening to the inner sphincter matters, because holding in for too long can damage the connection. When nerves and muscles stop hearing each other, the result is constipation.

It is fair to say we underestimate our gut. As Giulia Enders puts it, we are not just underrating it, we are ashamed of it, with more guilt than gut feeling.

Now, a simple question. How are you sitting? Not at your desk, but on the toilet. Many people scroll on a phone or read a book while sitting on the toilet.

That habit can lead to hemorrhoids, diverticulitis, and constipation, because the body tries to protect itself. A muscle wraps around the gut when you sit or stand, lending a hand to the sphincter team. A squatting posture opens a clean exit with a straight intestinal tract.

Think of using the toilet as the original mindfulness practice. Sit, breathe, and let go of what you no longer need.

You do not have to climb on top of the toilet to get good posture. When sitting, lean your upper body forward and rest your feet on a low stool in front of you. That small change is enough.

chapters.title: When things go off track
chapters.text:
You already know that listening to your sphincter helps prevent constipation, but what if you are in a good squatting posture and nothing happens for days? Try a few of the steps below.

Add more fiber. Whole grains, vegetables, nuts, and fruits all contain fiber, and you can also take a supplement.

Drink water. Steady hydration keeps your digestive system moving.

Go when you need to. Your gut loves routine, and so can you. Holding it in too long often leads to constipation.

Try probiotics and prebiotics. A pharmacist can point you to the right bacteria for a tired gut.

Sway gently. It sounds odd, but it can help. Try leaning forward and sitting back up while on the toilet.

The Bristol stool scale is a useful way to check whether your bowel movements are healthy. A well running digestive system produces stool that looks like a smooth, soft sausage or snake. If yours does not match, talk to a doctor.

Take care of your gut. After all, it takes care of you.

Sometimes the issue is a food intolerance, such as lactose, fructose, or gluten sensitivity. Your body is still adjusting to foods your ancestors never ate over millions of years, so it makes sense to experiment with how much milk or tropical fruit you consume and stick to smaller portions.

Vomiting is another way your bowels raise an alarm. Here are a few tips to keep nausea in check.

Look at the horizon when traveling. It helps the eyes and the balance sensors in your ears stay in sync.

Calm yourself. Music, lying on your side, deep breathing, or other relaxation methods can help, because nausea is often triggered by stress or anxiety.

Eat ginger root. It can help block the vomit center in the brain.

Visit a pharmacy. Several medicines are available, from ginger based options to those that numb the nerves in the stomach and gut.

Try the P6 pressure point. Doctors cannot fully explain why it works, but the point sits two to three finger widths below the wrist. Try gently pressing the skin to ease symptoms.

chapters.title: You carry your own inner world
chapters.text:
Many people think of bacteria as bad news, especially after the COVID-19 pandemic. After all, you came from a clean and protected place, the womb. The moment you are born, microbes arrive from the air and from everything around you. That does not mean babies belong in a bubble, because the first batch of gut microbes matters a great deal and helps protect you for years to come.

Roughly seventy percent of the immune cells in your body sit in your gut, so a healthy gut means a healthier body.

Your gut flora reaches a kind of adulthood around age three. Your microbes follow your meals closely and respond in different ways. A fun way to think about it: instead of "one spoonful for mommy," try "one spoonful for your microbes, who will dance while they digest."

Bacteria carry protein and produce small nutrients that the body absorbs easily. Microbes can also help explain why some people struggle to lose weight, and they can affect cholesterol levels. Lowering cholesterol is common, but very low cholesterol is not better. The aim is a healthy range that supports cell repair, sex hormones, and vitamin D.

On the whole, good bacteria defend the gut and help the villi grow, which lets the body absorb the minerals, nutrients, and vitamins it needs. A healthy microbiome supports the immune system, prevents diarrhea, and lowers the risk of allergies. The next key point covers which foods feed the good bacteria, and the Try this section gives you simple steps to treat your gut well.

chapters.title: Tend to your inner garden
chapters.text:
You may have heard of harmful bacteria like Salmonellae and Helicobacter pylori. To fight them and protect your inner world, you need helpful bacteria. Antibiotics, which literally mean "against life," are only needed in a narrow set of cases.

A better focus is on probiotics, which mean "for life." These are edible living bacteria that make you healthier and help refill your gut flora. You also need prebiotics, which mean "before life," because they feed the good bacteria.

Most bacteria are helpful, and around 95 percent of microbes are harmless. Even harmful bacteria, in small doses, train your immune system, so hand washing still matters.

Prebiotics are the quiet heroes of gut health, feeding the probiotics like fertilizer feeds a garden.

Many foods become more helpful to your gut once bacteria have broken them down. Milk becomes yogurt or kefir, and cabbage becomes sauerkraut. You can pick the fermented foods you enjoy, from ayran, soy sauce, kombucha, lassi, and fufu to kimchi.

Vegetables like asparagus, artichokes, green bananas, endive, Jerusalem artichoke, garlic, parsnips, onion, black salsify, wholegrain wheat, rye, oats, and leek are also great food for good bacteria. Let boiled potatoes or rice cool down, and the cooling creates resistant starches, which act like a prebiotic.

As Giulia Enders puts it, cleanliness in the gut is like cleanliness in a forest. Even the most dedicated cleaner would not drag a mop across the forest floor.

A simple home recipe: slice any vegetable, cabbage is a classic, and pack it into a jar. Add ten to fifteen grams of salt for every kilo of vegetables, mix well, and press it down firmly. Seal the jar tightly and leave it for one to four weeks. Your own jar of healthy bacteria is ready.

A small action you can take

Your bowels are part of a nervous system that shapes your mood. You can lift your health, your mood, and even your happiness with a few small steps. Add fermented foods to your day, such as yogurt, kimchi, kombucha, miso, or soy. Stay hydrated, but avoid sipping too much during meals. Chew each bite at least 20 to 30 times, and bring more fiber into your meals. Avoid antibiotics you do not need, and spend a few minutes a day on stress. Keep a simple food diary to spot your personal gut triggers. Small, steady steps add up.
